title: Evidence-Informed LLM Beliefs for Continual Scientific Discovery
abstract: Open-ended scientific discovery with large language models (LLMs) increasingly operates as a long-horizon loop of hypothesis search and verification, where a reward signal guides which hypotheses to test next. A notable recent example is AutoDiscovery, which uses "Bayesian surprise" - the belief shift an LLM undergoes after observing evidence for a hypothesis - as both a discovery metric and a reward for search. We first observe that AutoDiscovery treats surprisal as a static quantity, while surprisal in human reasoning is non-stationary - it is defined relative to beliefs that evolve with experience, a prerequisite for continual scientific discovery. We address this mismatch with evidence-informed LLM beliefs: priors updated with evidence from previous hypotheses to compute non-stationary surprisal for new hypotheses. We compare in-context belief-updating mechanisms and find that embedding-based retrieval-augmented generation over prior discoveries best anticipates eventual posteriors, identifying 37.5% of static surprisals as spurious. We then modify search to avoid these spurious rewards and prioritize hypotheses that remain surprising under non-stationary beliefs. Concretely, we introduce two complementary changes to the original search procedure: belief-update filtering and diversity maximization. Across five discovery domains, our method increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by 30.62% on average compared to the original search procedure, demonstrating that continual scientific discovery with LLMs requires not only better belief measurement but also search procedures that avoid redundancy and encourage diversity.

paper_title: Evidence-Informed LLM Beliefs for Continual Scientific Discovery

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to improve the process of continual scientific discovery using large language models (LLMs) by introducing evidence-informed LLM beliefs that update priors with evidence from previous hypotheses to compute non-stationary surprisal for new hypotheses.
objective.evidence: We address this mismatch with evidence-informed LLM beliefs: priors updated with evidence from previous hypotheses to compute non-stationary surprisal for new hypotheses.

knowledge_gap.answer: The paper addresses the gap in current LLM-driven discovery systems, which treat surprisal as a static quantity, failing to account for the non-stationary nature of human reasoning where beliefs evolve with experience.
knowledge_gap.evidence: We first observe that AutoDiscovery treats surprisal as a static quantity, while surprisal in human reasoning is non-stationary—it is defined relative to beliefs that evolve with experience, a prerequisite for continual scientific discovery.

answer: Introduction of non-stationary surprisal by updating LLM beliefs with evidence from previous hypotheses.
Development of belief-update filtering and diversity maximization mechanisms to improve hypothesis search.
Demonstration that embedding-based retrieval-augmented generation best anticipates eventual posteriors.
evidence: We introduce non-stationary surprisal, which evaluates hypotheses relative to LLM beliefs updated with evidence from previously tested hypotheses.
We present evidence-informed search for AutoDiscovery with belief-update filtering and diversity maximization.
We compare ICL-based belief-updating mechanisms and find that embedding-based RAG best anticipates posterior beliefs.

inspirational_papers.answer: - Agarwal et al. (2025b) AutoDiscovery uses Bayesian surprise as a reward for hypothesis search. (Methodological precursors)
inspirational_papers.evidence: A notable recent example of such a system is AutoDiscovery [Agarwal et al., 2025b], which uses 'Bayesian surprise' as a reward for hypothesis search.

steps.step: Introduce non-stationary surprisal by updating LLM beliefs with evidence from previous hypotheses.
steps.input: LLM priors and evidence from previous hypotheses.
steps.evidence: We introduce non-stationary surprisal, which evaluates hypotheses relative to LLM beliefs updated with evidence from previously tested hypotheses.

steps.step: Implement belief-update filtering to sample hypotheses that cannot be derived in-context using prior discoveries.
steps.output: Filtered hypotheses with potential for non-stationary surprisal.
steps.evidence: Belief-update filtering, to sample hypotheses that cannot be derived in-context using prior discoveries.

steps.step: Apply diversity maximization to prioritize exploration by selecting hypotheses with the least embedding-based similarity to previous discoveries.
steps.output: Diverse set of hypotheses for exploration.
steps.evidence: Diversity maximization, which explicitly prioritizes exploration by selecting hypotheses with the least embedding-based similarity to previous discoveries.

tools.name: AutoDiscovery
tools.description: Used for hypothesis search driven by Bayesian surprise.
tools.evidence: Our focus in this work is on a recent open-ended discovery system called AutoDiscovery.

tools.name: GPT-5-mini
tools.description: Used as the belief model for hypothesis generation and evaluation.
tools.evidence: We run belief elicitation on hypotheses generated by AutoDiscovery using GPT-5-mini as the belief model.

benchmark_datasets.name: DiscoveryBench
benchmark_datasets.data_description: Real-world domains including NLS-SES, Archaeology, Evolution Freshwater Fish.
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used for hypothesis generation and evaluation.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: We run belief elicitation on hypotheses generated by AutoDiscovery over 5 real-world domains from DiscoveryBench.

benchmark_datasets.name: BLADE
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used for hypothesis generation and evaluation.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: We run belief elicitation on hypotheses generated by AutoDiscovery over 5 real-world domains from BLADE.

evaluation_metrics.name: Non-stationary surprisal
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the shift in belief from prior to posterior after hypothesis verification.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate the effectiveness of evidence-informed belief updates.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: We measure performance using accumulated non-stationary surprisal, computed with Ctop-25 as the evidence-informed context representation.

methods.name: Hypothesis or Idea Generation
methods.description: The system produces candidate hypotheses or new research ideas from prior knowledge or external input.
methods.evidence: We prompt the LLM to generate testable hypotheses using domain-specific concepts derived from structured data.

methods.name: Experimental design generation
methods.description: The approach includes producing experimental protocols, configurations, or evaluation strategies.
methods.evidence: Our model proposes complete experimental setups including dataset split, evaluation metrics, and variables.

areas.name: Social Sciences
areas.description: The paper involves human expert studies in social science to validate the premise of non-stationary beliefs.
areas.evidence: We validate this premise with human experts in neuroscience and social science on hypotheses generated by AutoDiscovery.

areas.name: Applied Sciences & Engineering
areas.description: The proposed methods are applied to improve the process of scientific discovery using LLMs.
areas.evidence: Automated scientific discovery with large language models (LLMs) has begun showing promise in such continual settings.

performance_summary.summary: The proposed method increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by 30.62% on average compared to the original search procedure.
performance_summary.evidence: Across five discovery domains, our method increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by 30.62% on average compared to the original search procedure.

limitations.name: Dependence on LLM Samples
limitations.description: Belief estimates depend on prompting choices, sampling variance, and model calibration.
limitations.evidence: First, our belief estimates are elicited from LLM samples and therefore depend on prompting choices, sampling variance, calibration quality, and the model’s ability to faithfully express uncertainty.

limitations.name: Suppression of Revisitable Hypotheses
limitations.description: Non-stationary objective may suppress hypotheses worth revisiting under stronger evidence.
limitations.evidence: Second, while our non-stationary objective reduces redundant rediscovery, it may also suppress hypotheses that are legitimately worth revisiting under stronger or conflicting evidence.

limitations.name: Scalability to Open-ended Workflows
limitations.description: Scaling the method to open-ended workflows requires better evidence verification and long-term memory mechanisms.
limitations.evidence: Third, our experiments focus on controlled discovery settings with finite search budgets; scaling the method to open-ended scientific workflows will require better evidence verification, stronger uncertainty calibration, and mechanisms for long-term memory beyond the current context window.

future_directions.name: Explore Parametric Methods
future_directions.description: Investigate parametric methods for capturing the discovery context.
future_directions.evidence: Our study focuses on nonparametric methods to capture the discovery context, given the use of proprietary LLMs with only black-box access. Investigating parametric methods represents an important future direction.
